turns.user_message: I smoke about 15 cigarettes a day. And I drink on weekends. When I drink, the smoking goes up a lot. My wife has been asking me to stop both.
step2_rag_chunks: Avoid keeping cigarettes, lighters, or ashtrays nearby when trying to quit smoking.
Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.

Keep it simple, specific, and actionable.

step2_rag_chunks: Support from friends, family, or healthcare professionals can improve the chances of successfully quitting smoking.

Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.
Stress is a common trigger for smoking. Relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, meditation, or short walks can help manage stress without using tobacco.

Patient: My younger brother's been trying to quit smoking too. He's been through some stuff recently with his family, but he's always been a good listener. Maybe I could talk to him about quitting, and he could be with me when we're hanging out with friends.

Therapist: That's great that your brother is going through a similar experience and can offer support. How about you ask your brother to be your "quit buddy" and have him agree to not smoke around you, or to not encourage you to smoke when you're together, especially in social situations?
Patient: That's not a bad idea. I can ask him to be my quit buddy. But what about when I'm stressed at work? How can I deal with that?

Therapist: I can see you're feeling a bit overwhelmed with managing your stress at work. What if you took a few minutes each day to step outside for some fresh air, notice your surroundings, and focus on your breath, rather than reaching for a cigarette?

Patient: I can try that. It's not like I've never done it before, but maybe this time it'll stick. And if I'm really stressed, maybe I can take a short break and call my brother instead of grabbing a cigarette.
